narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 43% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, family background, and planned study schedule, focusing on patterns from past students without considering gender or race. The most influential factor pushing toward this higher failure risk was your choice to attend part-time, as the vast majority of students who passed—over 93%—were full-time, allowing more focused preparation that strongly correlates with bar success. In contrast, your Year 3 school decile ranking worked in your favor, placing you above the average for those who passed, signaling solid performance relative to peers. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 exceeded the typical 3.24 average among bar passers, giving the model confidence in your academic potential. Other strengths, like your LSAT score of 40 outperforming the passers' average of 37.27 and your upper-middle family income aligning with nearly half of successful students, helped pull the prediction downward but weren't enough to offset the part-time concern. Compared to typical admitted students, who often combine strong grades and test scores with full-time commitment, your profile is competitive academically but stands out for the part-time path, which the model sees as a bigger hurdle based on historical outcomes. This isn't a judgment on your abilities or background—it's a data-driven estimate to help the school select applicants most likely to thrive on the bar. To move forward, consider reapplying with a full-time plan if feasible, as that single change could dramatically shift your predicted odds. Strengthening your recent deciles or GPA through additional coursework might further bolster your case. You could also retake the LSAT to push even higher or explore programs with flexible scheduling that still support bar prep. Many students in similar situations succeed by addressing these key predictors—your solid foundation positions you well for another shot. Keep pursuing your goals; persistence often makes the difference.
